complete_examples.overview:
Worked examples demonstrating the vision discovery process with project-type-appropriate deliverable generation, state.json progress tracking, and multi-spawn resume. These show the interview rhythm — how to question, when to generate deliverables, how to handle distractions, and how resume works across spawns.
Examples cover multiple project types: GUI (HTML mocks), CLI (session transcripts). File paths shown are relative to the vision/ output directory.

example.name: task_management_app_full_discovery
example.scenario: An orchestrator spawns a Vision Hasher subtask: "Hash out the vision for a task management app. The user wants something like Trello but with built-in time tracking. vision-config.json specifies project_type=gui."

step.approach: VH reads vision-config.json → project_type=gui. Checks: vision/state.json does not exist → fresh start. Reads templates, parses brief, creates state.json. Presents plan with GUI phase names: "Foundation, Screens (interactive mocks), Flows, Edge Cases, Wire-Up. Ready?" User confirms.

Interviews through app-overview → personas → feature-inventory → data entities. 28 features across Core/Secondary/Nice-to-Have. Per-feature specs for core features. 7 data entities discovered. Phase 1 complete, committed, moving to Screens.

Delivers user priming. Interviews Dashboard using screen_decomposition. Generates screens/dashboard.md + screens/dashboard.html. User opens in browser, confirms. Placeholder pages generated for new navigation targets. Repeats for all 14 screens. Phase 2 complete.

Regenerates all 14 screen HTML files. Cross-reference check. Generates vision-summary.md with Screen Index, Feature Index, Data Entity Index, Flow Index. User walks through complete mock in browser. Approves.

key_takeaways.takeaway: vision-config.json drives project_type; state.json enables deterministic resume.
The interactive mock IS the confirmation surface — user confirms by clicking.
CSS fixation is the #1 derailment risk — redirect firmly to layout/behavior.
Placeholder pages must be generated immediately to prevent broken navigation links.

example.name: cli_tool_full_discovery
example.scenario: An orchestrator spawns VH: "Hash out the vision for 'dbmigrate' — a CLI database migration tool. vision-config.json specifies project_type=cli."

step.approach: VH reads vision-config.json → project_type=cli. Checks state.json → fresh start. Parses brief: "CLI database migration tool. Commands: init, create, migrate, rollback, status." Creates state.json with project_type="cli". Presents plan: "Foundation, Commands (spec + session transcripts), Workflows, Edge Cases, Wire-Up." User confirms.

Interviews app-overview → personas (DBAs, DevOps engineers, CI/CD pipelines) → feature-inventory (Migration Management, Rollback, Dry Run, Status Reporting, Config Management) → data entities (Migration, MigrationState, Config, Connection).

Delivers CLI priming script. Interviews `dbmigrate init` first. Uses command_decomposition pattern. Generates commands/init.md + commands/init.session.md. Transcript shows: $ dbmigrate init --db postgresql://localhost/mydb Created migrations/ directory with _template.sql Created dbmigrate.config.json $ dbmigrate init --help [help output]
Repeats for create, migrate, rollback, status. Each command gets paired .md + .session.md. User reviews transcripts, confirms behavior. 5 commands complete.
Defines workflows: "First Migration," "Rollback After Failed Deploy," "CI/CD Integration." Each workflow references commands and shows realistic terminal sessions with multiple commands chained.

Regenerates all session transcripts for consistency. Cross-reference check: every feature appears in command specs, every data entity referenced by commands. Generates vision-summary.md with Command Index (not Screen Index). User reviews complete spec. Approves.

key_takeaways.takeaway: CLI session transcripts are the equivalent of GUI HTML mocks — realistic output.
Every command gets paired .md + .session.md, same rigor as GUI screens.
Workflows show command chaining, piping, and multi-step sessions.
vision-config.json drives everything — VH adapts interview/de liverable automatically.

example.name: multi_spawn_resume
example.scenario: The user completed Phase 1 (Foundation) and Phase 2 (3 screens: Dashboard, Login, Settings) in the first spawn. The context window filled up. VH saved state.json with precise last_context and used attempt_completion. The orchestrator re-spawns VH to continue.

step.approach: VH reads vision/state.json: project_type=gui, current_phase="screens", phase_status="in_progress", completed.screens: dashboard (confirmed), login (confirmed), settings (in_progress), last_context: "Settings screen — discussing notification preferences section..." Cross-references: all files exist. Increments spawn_count. Presents resume status. User confirms → continues from push notification channels.

key_takeaways.takeaway: state.json + precise last_context = seamless resume across spawns.
The Phase 0 cross-reference catches disk/state drift before any work begins.

example.name: handling_a_stuck_user
example.scenario: The user can't decide how permissions should work. "I don't know, maybe admins can do everything and regular users can only see their own stuff?"

step.approach: VH: "Common permission models for task management apps: 1. Role-based: Admin (full access), Member (create/edit own...), Viewer (read-only) 2. Project-level: permissions set per project 3. Simple: one workspace, everyone sees everything Which model feels right?" User picks role-based with a fourth role. VH documents.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Never leave the user hanging with "what do you want?" — offer concrete options.
The options often spark new ideas the user hadn't considered.
